ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated hairy cucumber / fuzzy melon entry; closest defensible match is the general cucumber class FOODON_03301710. Hairy cucumber (Benincasa hispida var. chieh-qua) is botanically distinct from common cucumber (Cucumis sativus).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cucumber and gherkins) is the closest available category; hairy cucumber / fuzzy melon is not separately enumerated in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for hairy cucumber / fuzzy melon as a distinct food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw hairy cucumber is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium-sized whole fruit, commonly consumed as a snack or prepared as a vegetable side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Hairy Cucumber, Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hairy cucumber, raw (cr_id F14040049). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for hairy cucumber (Benincasa hispida var. chieh-qua) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other non-starchy cucurbit vegetables (e.g., cucumber GI ~15, zucchini GI ~15) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.45, rounded to 0.5. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing of hairy cucumber is not available in approved sources.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (250 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Hairy cucumber is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals on a DASH-style diet; its negligible sodium content and 250 mg/100 g potassium actively support blood pressure regulation, provided it is prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.0 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), and 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g minimising postprandial glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hairy cucumber is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its very low carbohydrate content, estimated low GI of approximately 15, and dietary fibre contribute to minimal postprandial blood glucose rise, and it may be consumed in generous portions as part of a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0 g/100 g confirmed), zero cholesterol, low sodium, and dietary fibre supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hairy cucumber poses no cardiovascular risk; its zero fat, zero cholesterol, very low sodium, and 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g make it a heart-healthy vegetable suitable for regular inclusion in a cardioprotective diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-nightshade vegetable with high water content and dietary fibre; no pro-inflammatory components identified
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hairy cucumber is generally well tolerated in rheumatoid arthritis as it is not a nightshade vegetable and contains no known pro-inflammatory components; however, it does not provide sufficient omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other established anti-inflammatory factors to be considered a therapeutic food for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hairy cucumber (Benincasa hispida var. chieh-qua), known in Chinese as 毛瓜 (mao gua) and in Malay as timun bulu, is a popular non-leafy vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. It is an elongated, green cucurbit fruit covered with fine white hairs on its skin, giving it a fuzzy appearance. The flesh is pale, firm, and crisp when raw, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and very high water content. Raw hairy cucumber is extremely low in energy (15 kcal/100 g), very low in carbohydrates (2.0 g/100 g) and fat (0 g/100 g confirmed naturally zero), and provides 2.1 g dietary fibre and 250 mg potassium per 100 g. Its negligible sodium content (2 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium level make it an ideal DASH-diet vegetable. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Clinically, hairy cucumber is appropriate for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management. It is commonly used in Chinese soups, stir-fries, and braised dishes, and may be eaten raw as a snack. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is hairy cucumber good for diabetes?
Is timun bulu good for blood pressure?
What is the GI of hairy cucumber?
Hairy cucumber vs regular cucumber nutrition
Can I eat hairy cucumber raw?
Is hairy gourd the same as hairy cucumber?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040049) with confidence 0.95. This is a canonical Singapore HPB entry for a commonly consumed vegetable. GI is an analogous estimate from approved peer-reviewed sources (Atkinson et al. 2008, University of Sydney GI Database) as no direct GI measurement exists for hairy cucumber.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero: hairy cucumber is a non-starchy vegetable that inherently contains no fat, confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo database (0.0 g/100 g). saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for hairy cucumber. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item as of 2026. GI of approximately 15 is an analogous estimate based on comparable non-starchy cucurbit vegetables (cucumber, zucchini) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing of hairy cucumber is not available in any approved source. The input record listed rheumatoid_arthritis suitable=false with a contradictory reason stating anti-inflammatory properties; this has been corrected to concern_level=low with an accurate clinical note reflecting that hairy cucumber is well tolerated but not a therapeutic anti-inflammatory food. The keto dietary tag from the input has been preserved in dietary_flags context given the very low carbohydrate content (2.0 g/100 g).
